questions.question: According to Newton's First Law of Motion, an object at rest tends to stay at rest unless acted upon by a net external force. Give an example from everyday life that supports this law.
questions.studentAnswer: A book placed on a table remains at rest until someone pushes it. This shows that an object at rest stays at rest unless acted upon by an external force.

questions.question: What is the relationship between acceleration and net force? Use Newton's Second Law of Motion in your answer.
questions.studentAnswer: According to Newton's Second Law of Motion, acceleration is directly proportional to the net force and inversely proportional to mass (F = ma). This means greater force produces greater acceleration for a given mass.
questions.feedback: Good job! You correctly explained Newton's Second Law of Motion and its relationship to acceleration and mass.

questions.question: A car accelerates uniformly from rest, reaching a speed of 30 m/s in 10 seconds. Calculate its average acceleration.
questions.studentAnswer: Initial velocity (u) = 0, final velocity (v) = 30 m/s, time (t) = 10 s. Acceleration (a) = (v - u) / t = 30 / 10 = 3 m/s^2.
questions.feedback: Well done! Your answer is correct and shows good understanding of Newton's Second Law.

questions.question: Inertia is the tendency of an object to resist changes in motion. Explain why it is easier to keep a moving ball rolling than to start it rolling from rest. Consider four different objects: a feather, a marble, a brick and a bowling ball. Each of these objects has been dropped from a height on Earth. Assume that the only force acting on each object is gravity. You are given the following information:
questions.studentAnswer: It is easier to keep a moving ball rolling than to start it because of inertia. An object at rest resists motion, while a moving object tends to continue moving. Heavier objects like a bowling ball have more inertia than lighter ones like a feather, so they resist changes in motion more strongly.
